front: What is the definition of an open set in a metric space?
back: A subset U of a metric space (X, d) is called open if, for every point x in U, there exists a real number ε > 0 such that the open ball B(x, ε) is entirely contained in U.

front: What are the three key properties of the collection of all open sets in a metric space?
back: 1. The empty set ∅ and the whole space X are open. 2. The union of any collection of open sets is open. 3. The intersection of any finite collection of open sets is open.

front: Is every open ball B(x, r) itself an open set?
back: Yes. For any point y in B(x, r), if we let ε = r - d(x,y) > 0, then the ball B(y, ε) is contained entirely within B(x, r). This proves B(x, r) is open.

front: What is the relationship between open sets and the topology of a metric space?
back: The collection of all open subsets of a metric space (X, d) forms a topology on X, called the metric topology. This topology is the foundation for defining continuity, convergence, and other topological concepts in the metric space.

front: How is an open set different from a closed set?
back: An open set contains a neighborhood around each of its points. A closed set is one whose complement is open. Equivalently, a closed set contains all its limit points. A set can be open, closed, both, or neither.
